Hmm, spoke too soon. I thought I'd be able to have `process()` pass the `data` to `commit()` but that doesn't work because we don't always commit at the end of `process()` -- we only commit once all the child nodes have been retrieved and committed. IOW, we need to keep (somewhere) the data received for every request, until that request is eventually committed. I could get rid of `request.data`, but then I'd have to store the data for every request in an instance variable of `HexaryTrieSync`. The `SyncResponse` you suggest would also have to be stored somewhere, and since it's disjoint from the actual request I'm not sure it gives us any guarantees really -- we could still try to commit a request for which there's no `SyncResponse` in the same way we can try to commit one which has `.data` set to None

Hmm, when we get a client error (anything between 201 to 499), it's probably a **programming bug**. But regardless, we still failed to get the user info from the server, and can't reliably tell if the user in question is an os-login user or not. In that case, shouldn't we fall back the to local user file as we do in the case of server errors?

In the current implementation, when we get a 3xx error for example, we'll skip the both if checks and go directly to `ParseJsonToEmail`, which will result in a crash: getting 3xx doesn't necessarily mean `response` will be empty, right?

So I think a more reliable logic should be:

* If we get a 200 with a non-empty response, great, it's an os-login user, go ahead and check its login permission
* If we get a 404, great still, it's *not* an os-login user, return PAM_SUCCESS
* In all other cases, we can't reliably tell, fall back to checking the local user's file

Again, this isn't quite right. In our Multithreaded upload implementation, we have reasonable confidence that we will always be either at the right offset (end of file) or behind the end of file (append already succeeded). But in the core logic, someone else could use our "append" implementation and incorrectly try to append past the end of the file. That will also result in a BadOffsetException, but if they retry we will silently continue, even though their data is not getting committed (since it is beyond the end of the file).

The right place to do this logic is inside of our Multithread.py implementation, where we have confidence in our offset management and can be explicit about how to handle badoffsetexceptions. We should not make a general statement of how badoffsetexceptions should be handled for all custom clients that use our core.py, since that can be surprising behavior.

Unfortunately, I think that this will break assumptions in a lot of Tasks. Whether invalidation is transitive or direct is currently a task-level concern. And in particular, "strict_deps" needs to only apply to tasks that explicitly ask for it.

As an example: `zinc` will use strict deps, and only pass strict deps to the compiler. But `junit` always uses the full transitive graph.

So I think that (with the current task API) it is necessary for this to be a change to this argument: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1e45167fab9a30f32207d025d91d2a071c8981fe/src/python/pants/task/task.py#L270 ... I definitely don't love that tasks are responsible for specifying this, but it's the way of things currently.

I'd prefer for the uniqueness criterion to be implemented one level up (in `_get_notebook_outputs`) rather than in this check. Here you're only getting the names themselves, the logic works whether you return one or many outputs. 

Importantly, by approaching it in this way we gain some forward compatibility. Even if we don't support multiple named outputs in a single cell today, if we were to begin to support that in the future, it won't require changing this API in order to enable that if it already returns an iterable. We'll then need to relax the uniqueness criterion in exactly one place (`_get_notebook_outputs`).

This is going in the _opposite_ direction from the way we should be going. This is _increasing_ the dependence on the Dist object to determine name/version/build. @bukepo I understand the optimization here and I agree it will helpful in some case, but I think it's not a good idea to change the behavior of the spinel C APIs for two reasons:

1) The `spinel.c` and `spinel.h` source files are directly integrated and used in other projects (e.g., in `wpantund` and some other internal projects). Changing this will have an impact on other projects.

2) While this makes the API more powerful, at the same time it becomes very error-prone and sort of dangerous. If the user/caller uses a local pointer variable (which is a very common use pattern) and forgets to initialize it to `NULL` then the data can be copied into some unknown/random memory region leading to memory corruption.  

I think it'd be safer to keep the C spinel APIs simple and safe and not change them.

I have been working on adding C++ wrapper classes over spinel C APIs (a `SpinelEncoder` and `SpinelDecoder`) to add more type-checking/safety to the code and help simplify `NcpBase` getter/setter methods. It's still work under progress (the `SpinelEncoder` code is in my OT fork in this (under-dev) branch https://github.com/abtink/openthread/commits/pr/spinel-encoder). We can consider adding support for such behavior in the C++ wrapper classes.

Okay, here's what this one should look like. I'll provide commentary after the code:

```python
    def it_provides_access_to_its_header(self, header_fixture):
        section, Header_, sectPr, header_ = header_fixture
        header = section.header
        Header_.assert_called_once_with(sectPr)
        assert header is header_
```

As you can see, it's pretty close for the first one :)

The main thing driving this structure is what the `Header` object itself is going to look like. The `Header` object will be an element proxy, but that element is actually optional, as we know. All we can tell for sure is that a `w:sectPr` element will be available and if the `headerReference` element is present, it will be a child of that `w:sectPr`. So the "sure thing", i.e. the `w:sectPr`, is what we pass to the constructor. Everything else is figured out by the `Header` object when and if it needs it. So the constructor is quite simple. You get back a `Header` object that knows what it's `w:sectPr` is. Everything else happens later.

I hear what you're saying, but here's my take:

- I believe it is standard practice (e.g. in Django Rest Framework) when checking row-level permissions, to check them against an instantiated model instance.
- I don't see a way around it, given that many permission checks rely on running methods or reading attributes on the model class.
- The same situation exists for reading (and updating, deleting, etc) -- there's nothing stopping a developer from doing stuff (e.g. reading some data, and doing something based on it) prior to checking the permissions. Care needs to be taken, and tests (e.g. of a view) can be written to check for violations, where appropriate.
- The majority of data access will happen through the HTTP API, from our frontend apps. In these cases, the developer will not be responsible for doing any checking, as it will be handled seamlessly by Django REST Framework.
It shouldn't be critical to proper use of the models -- the same stuff gets called from `save` as well, so non-validated use of the models still behaves well.

This wasn't part of this PR, but just a heads-up:

This method of combining numbers with translated strings won't work because it forces a certain order like

    ...english_text... {NUMBER} ...english_text... {NUMBER} ...english_text...

whereas in another language, it might be 

    {NUMBER} ... spanish_text ... {NUMBER}

In theory we should be using the [ICU Message Syntax](http://userguide.icu-project.org/formatparse/messages). However we don't yet have any working examples of this in use in the app. In the meantime, I'd recommend hard-coding these english strings and removing the `$tr` functionality because we don't want to set an incorrect precedent.
